bestPractices: Evaluate reward model on held-out pairs
Use ensemble of reward models
Monitor correlation between reward and human eval
Use adversarial evaluation

questions.question: What is the role of LoRA alpha (scaling) parameter?
questions.answer: LoRA alpha scales the low-rank update: W = W + (alpha/r) * B * A. It controls the adapter contribution magnitude. Effective LR of LoRA is proportional to alpha/r. Common: alpha=2*r (e.g., r=8, alpha=16). Higher alpha = faster learning, lower = more conservative.
questions.whyCorrect: Explains scaling mechanism, relationship to effective LR, and initialization rationale.
questions.realWorldExample: With r=8 and alpha=32: scaling factor 32/8=4. LoRA updates multiplied by 4 before adding, allowing larger effective updates.
commonMistakes: Alpha too high (unstable) or too low (too slow)
Not understanding alpha and LR interact
Changing alpha without adjusting LR
bestPractices: Start alpha=16 when r=8 (ratio=2)
Increase alpha/r for aggressive adaptation
Decrease for conservative (preventing forgetting)
Use lower alpha with RLHF/DPO

questions.question: How do you handle class imbalance in fine-tuning data?
questions.answer: Methods: (1) re-sampling - over-sample minority or under-sample majority; (2) weighted loss - higher loss weights for minority; (3) data augmentation - synthetic examples for underrepresented classes; (4) stratified splits; (5) evaluation - macro-F1 or balanced accuracy instead of accuracy.
questions.whyCorrect: Multiple complementary strategies at data, loss, evaluation, and post-processing levels.
questions.realWorldExample: Content moderation: 2% training data is toxic. Weighted loss + macro-F1 detects 80% of toxic content instead of 0%.
commonMistakes: Accuracy as sole metric with imbalance
Not addressing multi-class imbalance
Aggressive under-sampling losing signal
bestPractices: Use macro-F1 or balanced accuracy
Start with weighted loss
Use stratified cross-validation
For 1:1000+, combine over and under-sampling

questions.question: What is the difference between prompt loss masking and full sequence loss in fine-tuning?
questions.answer: Prompt loss masking sets prompt token loss to zero (labels=-100), so model only learns to predict response tokens. Full sequence loss computes on all tokens. Prompt masking is standard in instruction fine-tuning because it prevents wasting capacity on predicting the input.
questions.whyCorrect: Clearly differentiates and explains why prompt masking is preferred.
questions.realWorldExample: For 'Translate to French: Hello' / 'Bonjour': with masking, loss only on 'Bonjour'. Without, loss on all tokens - mostly evaluating input prediction.
commonMistakes: Not masking (3-10x less efficient)
Masking incorrectly (leaking response tokens)
Not using -100 label value
bestPractices: Always mask prompt tokens in SFT
Use labels= -100 for prompt positions
For causal LMs, shift labels
In seq-to-seq, masking is implicit

questions.question: How do you optimize the throughput of a fine-tuning run?
questions.answer: Optimize by: (1) maximize GPU utilization; (2) use BF16 mixed precision; (3) Flash Attention; (4) gradient checkpointing; (5) pack short sequences; (6) data loading with multiple workers and prefetching; (7) torch.compile; (8) profile with PyTorch profiler.
questions.whyCorrect: Covers major optimization dimensions: memory, precision, attention, data loading, profiling.
questions.realWorldExample: Team improved Llama-3-8B from 12K to 58K tokens/sec by: Flash Attention 2 (1.8x), BF16 (1.5x), packing (1.4x), 8 workers (1.2x), torch.compile (1.1x).
commonMistakes: Not profiling before optimizing
Too many data workers (CPU contention)
Forgetting pin_memory
Not using Flash Attention
bestPractices: Profile with nvidia-smi and PyTorch Profiler
Enable Flash Attention 2
Use BF16 on Ampere+
Pack sequences
Set num_workers=4-8
Try torch.compile

questions.question: How do you use LoRA target_modules selection?
questions.answer: target_modules specifies which linear layers get adapters. Common: (1) attention (q_proj, v_proj, k_proj, o_proj) - most impactful; (2) FFN (gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj) - for knowledge adaptation; (3) all linear - max capacity. Default: q_proj and v_proj.
questions.whyCorrect: Explains what target_modules controls and provides practical guidance.
questions.realWorldExample: Style adaptation: q_proj+v_proj (~16M params for 7B). Knowledge tasks: add FFN targets (2x params but better results).
commonMistakes: Not setting target_modules (default empty)
Targeting every layer unnecessarily
Not checking model naming conventions
bestPractices: Start with [q_proj, v_proj]
Add [k_proj, o_proj] for complex tasks
Add FFN for knowledge tasks
Check names with model.named_modules()

questions.question: How does NTK-aware or YaRN scaling work for extending context length?
questions.answer: NTK-aware and YaRN modify RoPE frequencies during fine-tuning to extend the effective context window. NTK-aware changes the base frequency of RoPE. YaRN uses wavelength-based scaling with a temperature factor. These allow fine-tuning on longer sequences (e.g., 4096 to 32768 tokens) without full re-training.
questions.whyCorrect: Correctly identifies these methods modify RoPE frequencies to extend context.
questions.realWorldExample: Model pre-trained with 4096 context needs 32K for legal documents. YaRN with scale factor 8 adjusts RoPE base frequency to handle 32K positions.
commonMistakes: Linear interpolation (loses high-frequency detail)
Not fine-tuning with new position encoding
Scale factor too high without fine-tuning
bestPractices: Use YaRN for RoPE-based models (LLaMA, Mistral)
Fine-tune at least 100-200 steps with extended context
Gradually increase context length
Monitor perplexity on long-range dependencies

questions.question: How does the PPO algorithm work in the context of RLHF?
questions.answer: PPO in RLHF: (1) policy generates responses; (2) frozen reward model scores each; (3) PPO computes advantages (how much better/worse vs average); (4) policy updated with clipped surrogate objective limiting per-step deviation; (5) KL penalty keeps close to SFT reference; (6) value function estimates expected reward for variance reduction.
questions.whyCorrect: Accurately describes PPO-actor-critic with clipping and KL penalty specific to RLHF.
questions.realWorldExample: Model generates 4 responses per prompt. Reward scores. PPO computes advantage = reward - value_prediction. If positive, increases log-probability (clipped epsilon=0.2). KL adds cost for divergence.
commonMistakes: No separate value function (high variance)
clip_epsilon too high or low
Not normalizing rewards
Too large PPO batch
bestPractices: Same-size value model
Normalize rewards per batch
Set clip_epsilon=0.2
Run 4-8 PPO epochs per rollout
Monitor KL (target 0.01-0.05 per update)

questions.question: What is the mathematical relationship between LoRA and full fine-tuning?
questions.answer: LoRA constrains weight updates to low-rank subspace: DeltaW = BA where A,B are rank-r. Gradients restricted to span of outer products of A and B. LoRA equals full FT only if optimal update has rank <= r. LoRA acts as implicit regularizer biasing toward low-rank solutions. Gradients: dL/dB = (dL/dW) * A^T and dL/dA = B^T * (dL/dW).
questions.whyCorrect: Describes mathematical constraints, gradient structure, and implicit regularization effect.
questions.realWorldExample: Rank-2 task: LoRA r=2 exactly matches full FT. Rank-64 task: LoRA r=8 still effective because real-world adaptation dominated by top singular components.
commonMistakes: Assuming LoRA can never match full FT
Not realizing higher r captures more singular components
Confusing update rank with needed gradient steps
bestPractices: Choose r based on expected adaptation rank
Use LoRA implicit regularization for small datasets
Effective degrees of freedom = r * (d_in + d_out)
At r=model_dim, LoRA equals full FT

questions.question: How do you design a reward model training pipeline for RLHF?
questions.answer: Pipeline: (1) collect preference pairs from diverse human raters; (2) initialize from SFT checkpoint with scalar head; (3) train with pairwise ranking loss (Bradley-Terry or cross-entropy); (4) regularize (weight decay, early stopping); (5) evaluate on held-out pairs (target accuracy 65-75%); (6) calibrate output distribution for stable PPO training.
questions.whyCorrect: Covers full pipeline from data collection through architecture, training, evaluation, calibration.
questions.realWorldExample: 100k preference pairs from diverse raters. Initialize from SFT, replace LM head with scalar. Train with binary cross-entropy. Evaluate on 10k held-out. Normalize scores mean=0, std=1 for PPO.
commonMistakes: Same distribution for training and evaluation
Not balancing annotator demographics
Too many epochs (overfitting)
Not calibrating scores
bestPractices: Diverse raters and prompts
Regularize with weight decay and early stopping
Evaluate on held-out
Calibrate scores (subtract mean, divide std)
Use ensemble for robustness

questions.question: What happens to the LoRA adapter when you merge it with the base model? Can you reverse the merge?
questions.answer: Merging computes W_new = W_base + (alpha/r) * B * A at each target layer, producing a single weight matrix. The merge is mathematically reversible only if you still have the original base model weights. Best practice: always save the unmerged adapter files separately.
questions.whyCorrect: Answers both parts precisely: merging is additive, reversible only with saved originals.
questions.realWorldExample: Merge LoRA into Llama-3-8B, then discover bug. If original adapter and base exist, fix and re-merge. If only merged model kept, cannot.
commonMistakes: Assuming merge is irreversible even with saved originals
Discarding adapter files after merge
Thinking merge modifies base model file
bestPractices: Always save adapter files before merging
Keep original base model weights unchanged
Use merge_and_unload only for deployment copies

questions.question: Your fine-tuned model performs well on validation but poorly in production. What could be wrong?
questions.answer: Possible causes: (1) distribution shift - production data differs from training; (2) validation leakage - val set too similar to training or used for early stopping; (3) metric mismatch - val metric not correlating with production quality; (4) overfitting from too many evaluation rounds; (5) production prompts differ in format/length/language.
questions.whyCorrect: Covers multiple common causes: data distribution, evaluation methodology, production issues.
questions.realWorldExample: Chatbot fine-tuned on clean typed queries gets 95% val accuracy. In production, users write 'plz fix my acct' with typos. Model fails because training data had no typos.
commonMistakes: Only checking most obvious cause
Not analyzing production vs training distributions
Assuming val metrics perfectly predict production
bestPractices: Create production-representative test set
Monitor production data distribution vs training
Implement gradual rollout (canary)
Set up production metrics monitoring

questions.question: Compare and contrast: full fine-tuning, LoRA, and adapter layers. When would you choose each?
questions.answer: Full FT updates all weights - highest capacity but expensive. LoRA uses low-rank attention matrices - efficient, matches quality for most tasks. Adapters are bottleneck networks between layers - modular and swappable. Choose full FT for maximum quality and budget. Choose LoRA for most practical tasks. Choose adapters for multiple task-specific modules sharing one base.
questions.whyCorrect: Correctly contrasts all three and provides clear decision criteria.
questions.realWorldExample: Startup with 50 clients: adapters (one per client, sharing one base). Researcher on single benchmark: full FT. Team for internal tool: LoRA.
commonMistakes: Confusing LoRA with adapters (different architectures)
Full FT when LoRA matches quality
Adapters when only one task
bestPractices: Full FT: when PEFT insufficient
LoRA: default for most
Adapters: multiple task-specific variants

questions.question: Your training loss goes down but validation loss goes up after 3 epochs. What do you do?
questions.answer: Classic overfitting. Actions: (1) stop training, use epoch 2 checkpoint; (2) increase regularization (weight decay, dropout); (3) reduce epochs with early stopping patience=2; (4) check val set representativeness; (5) if using LoRA, reduce rank or increase regularization; (6) check for data leakage between train and val.
questions.whyCorrect: Correctly identifies overfitting and provides prioritized action list.
questions.realWorldExample: Epoch 1: train 2.1/val 2.2. Epoch 2: train 1.5/val 1.8. Epoch 3: train 1.0/val 2.0. Solution: use epoch 2, add weight_decay=0.01, add early stopping.
commonMistakes: Ignoring validation loss and continuing
Assuming it will fix itself
Immediately changing architecture instead of simpler fixes
bestPractices: Use early stopping from start
Monitor both losses on same plot
Always keep best validation checkpoint
Investigate data quality before changing model

questions.question: You have a base model and a fine-tuned version. How do you determine if fine-tuning actually improved performance?
questions.answer: Run controlled comparison: (1) test both on same held-out evaluation set; (2) compute task-specific metrics for both; (3) compute delta; (4) check statistical significance (bootstrap CI); (5) evaluate on general benchmarks for forgetting; (6) run blind human evaluation if possible.
questions.whyCorrect: Comprehensive answer covering metrics, significance, forgetting checks, human evaluation.
questions.realWorldExample: Base gets BLEU=28, fine-tuned gets BLEU=34. Bootstrap CI [+4, +8] - significant improvement. But MMLU drops 68% to 62% - acceptable trade-off.
commonMistakes: Testing on training or validation data
Not checking statistical significance
Not evaluating forgetting
Non-representative test sets
bestPractices: Test on held-out data never seen during training
Use statistical tests (bootstrap, paired t-test)
Evaluate both task and general benchmarks
Run human evaluation for subjective tasks

questions.question: Walk me through how you would fine-tune a 70B model on a single consumer GPU with 24GB VRAM.
questions.answer: Use QLoRA: (1) load in 4-bit NF4; (2) enable double quantization; (3) use paged optimizers; (4) LoRA with r=16 on q_proj+v_proj; (5) gradient checkpointing; (6) batch size 1 with gradient accumulation (e.g., 16 steps = effective 16); (7) BF16 for adapters; (8) Flash Attention 2; (9) monitor memory and adjust.
questions.whyCorrect: Concrete step-by-step recipe using all memory optimizations.
questions.realWorldExample: Llama-3-70B on RTX 4090 (24GB): BitsAndBytes 4-bit, double_quant, paged_adamw_8bit, LoRA r=16, gradient_checkpointing=True, per_device_batch=1, gradient_accum=16, BF16. ~200 tokens/sec stable at ~22GB.
commonMistakes: Trying full fine-tuning (impossible, >140GB)
Forgetting paged optimizers (OOM)
Not enabling double quantization
Batch size too high
bestPractices: Use QLoRA for 70B models
All memory optimizations together
Monitor with nvidia-smi
Start with small experiments before full run
Consider NF4 + double_quant + paged optimizers

questions.question: What does RAG stand for in the context of LLMs?
questions.answer: RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation, a technique that enhances LLM outputs by retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge base and incorporating them into the prompt before generation.
questions.whyCorrect: RAG explicitly combines retrieval of external documents with the generative capabilities of LLMs, hence the name Retrieval-Augmented Generation.

whyOthersIncorrect.Retrieval-Associated Generation: Not an official term; misrepresents the augmentation aspect.
whyOthersIncorrect.Random Access Generation: Conflates retrieval with random memory access, which is inaccurate.
whyOthersIncorrect.Reinforced AI Generation: Confuses RAG with reinforcement learning techniques.

questions.realWorldExample: A customer support bot that searches a company's knowledge base for relevant articles and uses them to generate accurate answers.
commonMistakes: Confusing RAG with simple web search
Assuming RAG replaces the need for an LLM entirely
bestPractices: Use RAG when you need up-to-date or domain-specific knowledge beyond the LLM's training data
Ensure the retrieval component returns high-quality, relevant documents

questions.question: Why is RAG preferred over simply using a pre-trained LLM for knowledge-intensive tasks?
questions.answer: RAG provides access to external, up-to-date, and domain-specific knowledge that the LLM may not have in its training data. It reduces hallucinations by grounding responses in retrieved documents, enables traceability through source attribution, and avoids the cost and latency of retraining or fine-tuning the model.
questions.whyCorrect: RAG directly addresses the key limitations of LLMs: knowledge cutoff, hallucination, and lack of domain-specific data by augmenting generation with retrieved evidence.

whyOthersIncorrect.RAG makes LLMs faster: RAG typically adds latency due to the retrieval step.
whyOthersIncorrect.RAG replaces the LLM's parameters: RAG does not modify model weights; it augments the input.
whyOthersIncorrect.RAG eliminates the need for embeddings: RAG relies on embeddings for retrieval, so embeddings are still required.

questions.realWorldExample: A legal research assistant that retrieves case law from a continuously updated database rather than relying on the LLM's training data cutoff.
commonMistakes: Assuming RAG is always better than fine-tuning for every use case
Ignoring the quality of retrieved documents as a critical factor
bestPractices: Use RAG when the knowledge base changes frequently
Combine RAG with prompt engineering for optimal results

questions.question: What are the two core components of a RAG pipeline?
questions.answer: The two core components are: (1) the Retriever, which fetches relevant documents or passages from an external knowledge base given a query, and (2) the Generator, which is an LLM that synthesizes an answer using the retrieved context along with the original query.
questions.whyCorrect: RAG explicitly separates the retrieval step from the generation step, combining both to produce grounded outputs.

whyOthersIncorrect.Retriever + Classifier: Classification is not a core RAG component; the second component is generation.
whyOthersIncorrect.Encoder + Decoder: This describes the internal architecture of transformer models, not RAG's pipeline components.
whyOthersIncorrect.Indexer + Ranker: These are sub-components of the retriever, not the two main pipeline stages.

questions.realWorldExample: A medical QA system where the retriever pulls relevant clinical guidelines and the LLM generates a physician-friendly summary.
commonMistakes: Conflating indexing with retrieval
Forgetting that both components must be tuned together
bestPractices: Optimize the retriever for recall to ensure relevant docs are surfaced
Provide clear instructions to the generator for how to use retrieved context

questions.question: How does RAG differ from prompt engineering?
questions.answer: Prompt engineering involves crafting input prompts to guide an LLM's existing knowledge, while RAG dynamically retrieves external documents to augment the prompt with factual context. Prompt engineering is limited to what the model already knows; RAG extends capabilities by incorporating information the model was never trained on.
questions.whyCorrect: Prompt engineering manipulates how existing knowledge is accessed, while RAG introduces entirely new external information into the generation process.

whyOthersIncorrect.They are the same thing: Prompt engineering alone cannot introduce information outside the model's training data.
whyOthersIncorrect.RAG does not use prompts: RAG heavily relies on prompt engineering to structure how retrieved context is used.
whyOthersIncorrect.Prompt engineering is more powerful: RAG can access real-time data that prompt engineering cannot.

questions.realWorldExample: Asking an LLM to explain quantum physics via prompt engineering uses its training data; RAG retrieves a latest research paper on quantum computing for a more current answer.
commonMistakes: Thinking RAG eliminates the need for prompt engineering
Assuming prompt engineering can substitute for retrieval of external data
bestPractices: Combine prompt engineering with RAG for best results
Use prompt engineering to improve retrieval query quality

questions.question: What is the role of overlap in chunking strategies?
questions.answer: Overlap ensures that adjacent chunks share some common text, preventing the loss of context at chunk boundaries. Without overlap, information that spans two chunks (e.g., a sentence split across a boundary) may be missed by the retriever. Typical overlap is 10-20% of the chunk size. Overlap improves retrieval completeness but increases storage and embedding costs.
questions.whyCorrect: Overlap compensates for the boundary problem in chunking by ensuring context continuity between adjacent chunks.

whyOthersIncorrect.Overlap is unnecessary: Without overlap, important context at boundaries is lost.
whyOthersIncorrect.More overlap is always better: Excessive overlap increases storage and may introduce redundancy.
whyOthersIncorrect.Overlap only applies to fixed-size chunk: Overlap can be applied to various chunking strategies.

questions.realWorldExample: A 500-token chunk with 50-token overlap ensures that a sentence split at token 480 is also captured in the next chunk starting at token 450.
commonMistakes: Using zero overlap and losing boundary context
Using too much overlap and inflating index size
bestPractices: Use 10-20% overlap as a starting point
Evaluate retrieval with and without overlap for your specific data

questions.question: What is document loading in a RAG pipeline, and why is it important?
questions.answer: Document loading is the process of ingesting raw documents (PDFs, HTML, Markdown, databases, etc.) into the RAG pipeline and converting them into a format suitable for chunking and indexing. It is critical because the quality and completeness of loaded documents directly determine the quality of retrieval and downstream generation.
questions.whyCorrect: Document loading is the first step in any RAG pipeline, transforming heterogeneous source documents into a uniform format for downstream processing.

whyOthersIncorrect.Document loading is the same as chunking: Loading ingests documents; chunking splits them. They are distinct steps.
whyOthersIncorrect.Loading only applies to PDFs: Loading handles many formats: HTML, CSV, Markdown, databases, APIs, etc.
whyOthersIncorrect.It is not important in RAG: The quality of loading directly impacts retrieval quality.

questions.realWorldExample: Using LangChain's DirectoryLoader to load a folder of mixed-format documents (PDFs, Word docs, HTML) into a unified format for processing.
commonMistakes: Ignoring metadata during loading
Not handling format-specific parsing errors gracefully
bestPractices: Use robust parsers that preserve document structure
Extract and store metadata (source, date, author) alongside content

questions.question: What is a vector database and why is it needed in RAG?
questions.answer: A vector database is a specialized data store designed to index, store, and query high-dimensional vector embeddings efficiently. In RAG, it enables fast similarity search over potentially millions of document embeddings to find the most relevant chunks for a query. Traditional databases are not optimized for high-dimensional similarity search, making vector databases essential for scalable RAG.
questions.whyCorrect: Vector databases provide the indexing structures (e.g., HNSW, IVF) needed for efficient approximate nearest neighbor search over embedding vectors at scale.

whyOthersIncorrect.A regular SQL database suffices: Standard databases lack efficient ANN search for high-dimensional vectors.
whyOthersIncorrect.Vector databases store raw text: They primarily store embeddings; text is stored alongside or separately.
whyOthersIncorrect.Vector databases are only needed for sma: Even small datasets benefit from optimized vector search, but they become essential at scale.

questions.realWorldExample: Pinecone indexing 10 million product description embeddings and retrieving the top-5 most similar products for a customer query in under 100ms.
commonMistakes: Using brute-force search instead of a vector DB at scale
Choosing a vector DB based only on features without considering operational complexity
bestPractices: Evaluate vector databases on your data size, latency, and cost requirements
Consider managed services vs self-hosted for operational simplicity

questions.question: What are embedding models and what role do they play in RAG?
questions.answer: Embedding models convert text (queries and document chunks) into dense numerical vectors (embeddings) in a high-dimensional space. In RAG, they are critical because they enable semantic search: chunks with similar meaning to a query are close in embedding space. The retriever uses these embeddings to find the most relevant documents for a given query.
questions.whyCorrect: Embedding models are the foundation of semantic retrieval in RAG, transforming text into a form that enables similarity-based search.

whyOthersIncorrect.Embedding models generate text: Embedding models produce vectors; generation is done by the LLM.
whyOthersIncorrect.Embeddings are only used during indexing: Embeddings are used for both indexing chunks and encoding queries at retrieval time.
whyOthersIncorrect.All embedding models are the same: Different models vary in dimensionality, quality, and domain specialization.

questions.realWorldExample: Using OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small to encode both user queries and document chunks into 1536-dimensional vectors for cosine similarity search.
commonMistakes: Using the same embedding model for indexing and querying without consistency
Choosing an embedding model without evaluating on domain-specific data
bestPractices: Use the same embedding model for indexing and querying
Benchmark embedding models on your domain data before committing

questions.question: What is chunking in RAG and why can't we just index entire documents?
questions.answer: Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller, manageable segments (chunks) before embedding and indexing. We cannot index entire documents because: (1) embedding models have token limits, (2) large documents dilute semantic signal, (3) retrieval requires precise matching of queries to relevant passages rather than whole documents, and (4) LLM context windows have finite capacity.
questions.whyCorrect: Chunking addresses practical constraints of embedding models and LLM context windows while improving retrieval precision.

whyOthersIncorrect.Chunking is optional: Without chunking, retrieval quality and context utilization degrade significantly.
whyOthersIncorrect.Larger chunks are always better: Overly large chunks reduce retrieval precision and may exceed context limits.
whyOthersIncorrect.Chunking is only needed for large files: Even small documents benefit from chunking for precise retrieval.

questions.realWorldExample: A 50-page legal document split into sections per clause, where each clause is a chunk, allowing retrieval of only the relevant clause for a specific query.
commonMistakes: Using fixed-size chunking without considering document structure
Making chunks too small and losing context
bestPractices: Chunk based on document structure (headings, paragraphs)
Test different chunk sizes for your specific use case

questions.question: What is the main difference between dense retrieval and sparse retrieval?
questions.answer: Dense retrieval uses continuous vector embeddings to find semantically similar documents based on meaning. Sparse retrieval uses discrete term-based representations (like BM25 or learned sparse models) that match exact or close keyword occurrences. Dense retrieval excels at semantic understanding; sparse retrieval excels at precise keyword matching and is more interpretable.
questions.whyCorrect: Dense and sparse retrieval represent fundamentally different approaches to matching queries to documents: semantic similarity vs. term frequency.

whyOthersIncorrect.They always return identical results: They often return different results, especially for queries with vocabulary mismatch.
whyOthersIncorrect.Sparse retrieval is always faster: Modern dense retrieval with HNSW can be equally fast.
whyOthersIncorrect.Dense retrieval is always more accurate: Sparse retrieval often outperforms for keyword-heavy or exact-match queries.

questions.realWorldExample: Dense retrieval matching 'canine nutrition' to 'dog food', while sparse retrieval requires the term 'canine' to appear in the document.
commonMistakes: Using only one retrieval method when hybrid would be better
Assuming dense retrieval always produces better results
bestPractices: Consider hybrid approaches that combine both
Test both methods on your specific query patterns

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a vector database?
questions.answer: FAISS is a C++ library for efficient similarity search, not a vector database. It provides indexing algorithms (HNSW, IVF) but lacks persistence, querying APIs, metadata management, and operational features that databases like Pinecone, Weaviate, and Qdrant provide.
questions.whyCorrect: FAISS is fundamentally a library of similarity search algorithms, not a complete database system with storage, API, and management features.

whyOthersIncorrect.FAISS is a vector database: FAISS is a library, not a database with persistence and management.
whyOthersIncorrect.ChromaDB is a vector database: ChromaDB is a lightweight vector database for prototyping.
whyOthersIncorrect.pgvector is not a vector database: pgvector extends PostgreSQL with vector search capabilities.

questions.realWorldExample: A research team using FAISS for experimentation in Jupyter notebooks, then deploying on Qdrant for production serving.
commonMistakes: Confusing FAISS with a full vector database solution
Assuming all vector similarity tools are databases
bestPractices: Understand the difference between vector libraries and vector databases
Use FAISS for experimentation; evaluate vector DBs for production

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'top-k' parameter in RAG retrieval?
questions.answer: Top-k determines how many of the most relevant chunks to retrieve from the vector database. A higher k returns more context (potentially more complete answers) but uses more LLM tokens and may include less relevant chunks. A lower k is faster and cheaper but may miss relevant information. Typical values range from 3-10 depending on the use case and context window.
questions.whyCorrect: Top-k directly controls the trade-off between retrieval completeness and context window usage/cost.

whyOthersIncorrect.Top-k determines chunk size: Top-k determines how many chunks are retrieved, not their size.
whyOthersIncorrect.Higher k always improves answers: Higher k may include irrelevant chunks that dilute the context.
whyOthersIncorrect.Top-k is only used at indexing time: Top-k is a query-time parameter controlling retrieval quantity.

questions.realWorldExample: A medical QA system using top-k=5 to balance between having enough clinical context and staying within the LLM's context window.
commonMistakes: Setting top-k too high and wasting context on irrelevant chunks
Setting top-k too low and missing critical information
bestPractices: Start with top-k=5 and adjust based on evaluation
Monitor how answer quality changes with different k values

questions.question: What role does a system prompt play in a RAG pipeline?
questions.answer: The system prompt in RAG defines the LLM's behavior, including: (1) instructions to answer only from provided context, (2) how to handle questions the context cannot answer, (3) formatting requirements for citations, (4) the assistant's persona and expertise level, and (5) constraints on what information to include or exclude. A well-crafted system prompt is essential for grounding and reducing hallucination.
questions.whyCorrect: The system prompt instructs the LLM on how to use retrieved context, when to abstain, and how to present answers—these are not default LLM behaviors.

whyOthersIncorrect.System prompts are optional for RAG: Without grounding instructions, the LLM may hallucinate regardless of retrieved context.
whyOthersIncorrect.System prompts and user prompts are the: System prompts set persistent behavior; user prompts contain specific queries.
whyOthersIncorrect.Any generic prompt works for RAG: RAG-specific instructions for grounding and citation significantly improve output quality.

questions.realWorldExample: A system prompt stating 'You are a helpful assistant. Answer ONLY using the provided context. If the context is insufficient, say I cannot answer this question.'
commonMistakes: Not including 'answer only from context' instructions
Using generic prompts without RAG-specific grounding instructions
bestPractices: Always include grounding instructions
Test prompts with adversarial queries that should NOT be answerable from context

questions.question: What is the difference between a retriever and a reranker in RAG?
questions.answer: A retriever finds the initial set of potentially relevant documents from the full index using fast approximate search (e.g., vector similarity or BM25). A reranker takes this initial set and re-orders them using a more accurate but slower model (typically a cross-encoder) that jointly considers the query and each document. Retrieval prioritizes recall; reranking prioritizes precision.
questions.whyCorrect: Retrieval is a fast, broad search for candidates; reranking is a slow, precise re-ordering of those candidates.

whyOthersIncorrect.They are the same component: Retrievers search the full index; rerankers re-order a small candidate set.
whyOthersIncorrect.Rerankers replace retrievers: Rerankers process the output of retrievers; they don't replace them.
whyOthersIncorrect.Reranking is always faster than retrieva: Reranking is slower per document but operates on a much smaller set.

questions.realWorldExample: A vector database retrieves top-50 candidates, then Cohere Rerank re-scores and selects the top-5 most relevant chunks.
commonMistakes: Confusing the roles of retriever and reranker
Skipping reranking evaluation to see if it adds value
bestPractices: Use reranking when retrieval precision is insufficient
Evaluate reranking latency vs quality improvement

questions.question: How does cosine similarity work in RAG retrieval?
questions.answer: Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two embedding vectors, producing a score between -1 and 1. A score of 1 means identical direction (same meaning), 0 means orthogonal (unrelated), and -1 means opposite. In RAG, the query embedding is compared to all document embeddings using cosine similarity, and the highest-scoring documents are retrieved. It is preferred over Euclidean distance for high-dimensional embeddings because it focuses on direction (semantic meaning) rather than magnitude.
questions.whyCorrect: Cosine similarity captures semantic similarity by measuring the angle between vectors, making it ideal for high-dimensional text embeddings.

whyOthersIncorrect.Cosine similarity measures vector length: It measures the angle between vectors, not their magnitude.
whyOthersIncorrect.Euclidean distance is always better: Cosine similarity is generally preferred for text embeddings because it focuses on direction.

questions.realWorldExample: A query embedding for 'healthy dog food' having cosine similarity of 0.92 with a chunk about 'nutritious canine diet' but only 0.3 with a chunk about 'car repair'.
commonMistakes: Confusing cosine similarity with Euclidean distance
Assuming all distance metrics are interchangeable for embeddings
bestPractices: Use cosine similarity as the default for text embeddings
Understand when other metrics (dot product, L2) might be more appropriate

questions.question: What is a RAG evaluation dataset and why do you need one?
questions.answer: A RAG evaluation dataset contains pairs of queries with expected answers and ideally expected retrieved documents. It is needed to: (1) measure retrieval quality (precision@k, recall@k), (2) measure generation quality (faithfulness, relevance), (3) compare different RAG configurations objectively, (4) detect regressions when making changes, and (5) establish performance baselines. Without an evaluation dataset, RAG improvements are based on intuition rather than measurable evidence.
questions.whyCorrect: An evaluation dataset provides the ground truth needed to objectively measure and improve RAG system quality.

whyOthersIncorrect.Evaluation datasets are only needed for: Production RAG systems need them for quality assurance and regression testing.
whyOthersIncorrect.Manual testing is sufficient: Manual testing doesn't scale and isn't reproducible.
whyOthersIncorrect.Any dataset can be used for evaluation: Evaluation datasets need queries with known correct answers.

questions.realWorldExample: A team building 500 query-answer pairs from real support tickets to evaluate their RAG chatbot's accuracy before each deployment.
commonMistakes: Not building an evaluation dataset before starting development
Using the same data for evaluation and testing (overfitting)
bestPractices: Build evaluation datasets from real user queries
Keep evaluation datasets separate from training/fine-tuning data

questions.question: What is the purpose of metadata in a RAG vector store?
questions.answer: Metadata provides additional structured information attached to each chunk (e.g., source document, date, category, author, section title). It serves multiple purposes: (1) filtering retrieval results at query time, (2) enabling the LLM to cite sources accurately, (3) supporting debuggability by tracing answers back to source documents, (4) enabling time-based or category-based queries, and (5) improving retrieval precision through hybrid filtering.
questions.whyCorrect: Metadata enriches vector entries with structured information that enables filtering, citation, debugging, and improved retrieval precision.

whyOthersIncorrect.Metadata is only stored, never queried: Metadata is actively used for filtering at query time.
whyOthersIncorrect.Embeddings contain all necessary informa: Embeddings capture semantic meaning; metadata captures structured attributes.
whyOthersIncorrect.Metadata increases retrieval latency sig: Well-indexed metadata filters have minimal performance impact.

questions.realWorldExample: A support bot filtering retrieved chunks to only the 'billing' category for payment-related questions, with source document URLs for citation.
commonMistakes: Not attaching metadata during index building
Storing too much metadata that isn't useful for retrieval
bestPractices: Design your metadata schema before building the index
Include source, date, and category as standard metadata fields

questions.question: What is the difference between RAG and simply passing a long prompt with information to an LLM?
questions.answer: RAG differs from long-prompt injection in that: (1) RAG dynamically selects relevant documents at query time from a large knowledge base, while long prompts require manually or programmatically selecting content, (2) RAG scales to millions of documents via indexing, while long prompts are limited by context window, (3) RAG provides retrieval traceability and metadata, while long prompts lack search precision, and (4) RAG can use specialized retrieval algorithms (semantic search, BM25, hybrid) to find the most relevant content automatically.
questions.whyCorrect: RAG automates retrieval from large document collections at query time using optimized search, while long prompts require manual content selection and are limited by context window.

whyOthersIncorrect.They are functionally identical: RAG provides automated, scalable retrieval; long prompts are manual and limited.
whyOthersIncorrect.Long prompts are always better because t: Long prompts waste tokens on irrelevant content; RAG retrieves only what's needed.
whyOthersIncorrect.RAG requires more complex infrastructure: RAG's complexity is justified by scalability and retrieval quality.

questions.realWorldExample: Instead of manually copying 10 relevant paragraphs into a prompt, RAG automatically searches 10,000 documents and retrieves the 5 most relevant paragraphs.
commonMistakes: Confusing manual context injection with RAG
Underestimating RAG's value for large knowledge bases
bestPractices: Use RAG when knowledge exceeds what can fit in a prompt
Recognize that automated retrieval outperforms manual content selection at scale

questions.question: What is indexing in a vector database and why is it important?
questions.answer: Indexing is the process of organizing vector embeddings into data structures (e.g., HNSW graphs, IVF partitions) that enable fast approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search. Without indexing, finding similar vectors would require brute-force comparison against every vector in the database—impractical at scale. Indexing trades a small amount of accuracy (approximate results) for dramatic speed improvements, enabling sub-second search over millions of vectors.
questions.whyCorrect: Vector indexing structures enable fast similarity search by avoiding brute-force comparison, making large-scale RAG feasible.

whyOthersIncorrect.Indexing is the same as embedding genera: Indexing organizes embeddings; embedding generation creates them.
whyOthersIncorrect.Indexing provides exact nearest neighbor: Most indexing algorithms provide approximate results for speed.
whyOthersIncorrect.Indexing is only needed for large datase: Even moderate datasets benefit from indexing for latency reasons.

questions.realWorldExample: HNSW indexing enabling search over 1 million embeddings in under 10ms, compared to seconds for brute-force search.
commonMistakes: Assuming indexing provides exact results
Choosing an index type without understanding its trade-offs
bestPractices: Use HNSW as a default indexing algorithm
Understand the recall-latency trade-off of different index types

questions.question: How does the choice of LLM affect RAG system quality?
questions.answer: The LLM choice affects RAG quality in several ways: (1) Larger models better synthesize and reason over retrieved context, (2) Models with larger context windows accommodate more retrieved chunks, (3) Instruction-tuned models better follow grounding instructions, (4) Some models are more prone to hallucination than others, (5) Model-specific tokenization affects how much retrieved content fits in the context window, and (6) Response quality varies based on how well the model handles the specific domain of the knowledge base.
questions.whyCorrect: The LLM is the generator component of RAG; its capabilities in instruction following, reasoning, and context utilization directly determine answer quality.

whyOthersIncorrect.The LLM doesn't matter if retrieval is g: Even with perfect retrieval, a poor LLM may hallucinate or misinterpret context.
whyOthersIncorrect.Always use the largest available model: Larger models are more expensive and slower; smaller models may suffice for simple tasks.
whyOthersIncorrect.All LLMs handle RAG equally well: Instruction tuning and context handling vary significantly across models.

questions.realWorldExample: A company finding that GPT-4 provides better synthesis of legal documents than GPT-3.5-turbo, justifying the higher cost for their legal RAG use case.
commonMistakes: Defaulting to the cheapest LLM without evaluating quality
Not testing multiple LLMs on the specific RAG evaluation dataset
bestPractices: Benchmark multiple LLMs on your RAG evaluation dataset
Balance model capability with latency and cost requirements

questions.question: How does RAG compare to fine-tuning for updating an LLM's knowledge?
questions.answer: RAG retrieves fresh, external information at inference time without modifying model weights, making it ideal for frequently changing data. Fine-tuning permanently updates model parameters with new data, which is better for behavior/style changes but expensive and slow for knowledge updates. RAG is more cost-effective and transparent for knowledge-intensive tasks, while fine-tuning is better for task adaptation.
questions.whyCorrect: RAG dynamically accesses external knowledge at query time while fine-tuning modifies the model itself. They serve different primary purposes.

whyOthersIncorrect.Fine-tuning is always cheaper: Fine-tuning requires GPU resources and dataset preparation, often more expensive than RAG.
whyOthersIncorrect.RAG permanently changes the model: RAG does not modify model weights; it augments inputs.
whyOthersIncorrect.They are interchangeable: RAG and fine-tuning address different problems and can even be combined.

questions.realWorldExample: A news aggregator uses RAG to surface today's articles rather than fine-tuning daily. A chatbot company fine-tunes for a brand's tone of voice.
commonMistakes: Choosing fine-tuning when the goal is up-to-date knowledge
Ignoring that RAG and fine-tuning can be complementary
bestPractices: Use RAG for knowledge that changes frequently
Use fine-tuning for style, tone, or task-specific behavior changes

questions.question: What are the main chunking strategies available for RAG?
questions.answer: Main chunking strategies include: (1) Fixed-size chunking by character/token count with optional overlap, (2) Recursive character splitting that respects structural delimiters, (3) Semantic chunking that splits at topic boundaries using embeddings, (4) Document-structure-based chunking using headings, paragraphs, or sections, (5) Agentic chunking that uses an LLM to determine meaningful split points, and (6) Parent-child chunking where small chunks are retrieved but larger parent context is returned.
questions.whyCorrect: Each strategy offers different trade-offs between simplicity, semantic coherence, and retrieval quality. The optimal choice depends on document structure and use case.

whyOthersIncorrect.Only fixed-size chunking exists: Multiple strategies exist, each with distinct trade-offs.
whyOthersIncorrect.Semantic chunking is always best: It is more computationally expensive and may not suit all document types.
whyOthersIncorrect.Chunking strategy does not affect retrie: It significantly impacts retrieval precision and answer quality.

questions.realWorldExample: Wikipedia articles use structure-based chunking (sections), while a chatbot over raw text might use recursive character splitting.
commonMistakes: Defaulting to fixed-size without testing alternatives
Ignoring document structure when choosing a strategy
bestPractices: Experiment with multiple strategies and evaluate retrieval quality
Use semantic chunking for unstructured text and structural chunking for well-formatted documents

questions.question: Compare OpenAI embeddings, sentence-transformers, and Cohere embeddings for RAG.
questions.answer: OpenAI embeddings (text-embedding-3-small/large) are API-based, high quality, 1536/3072 dimensions but require API calls and incur costs. Sentence-transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) are open-source, self-hostable, free at inference, with various size trade-offs. Cohere embeddings offer multilingual support with specialized models. Choice depends on cost tolerance, privacy requirements, and language needs.
questions.whyCorrect: Each embedding provider has distinct trade-offs across hosting, cost, performance, and features.

whyOthersIncorrect.OpenAI embeddings are always the best: Best depends on constraints like cost, latency, privacy, and language requirements.
whyOthersIncorrect.Sentence-transformers cannot match API q: Fine-tuned sentence-transformers can match or exceed API models on domain data.
whyOthersIncorrect.Cohere is only for non-English: Cohere offers strong English performance alongside multilingual support.

questions.realWorldExample: A healthcare startup uses sentence-transformers self-hosted for HIPAA compliance, while a prototyping team uses OpenAI embeddings for speed-to-market.
commonMistakes: Choosing embeddings based only on benchmarks, not real data
Ignoring latency and cost implications of API-based embeddings
bestPractices: Benchmark all three on your specific dataset and query patterns
Consider self-hosted options for sensitive data

questions.question: What is semantic chunking and how does it differ from fixed-size chunking?
questions.answer: Semantic chunking uses embeddings to detect topic or semantic boundaries within a document, splitting text where the meaning changes significantly. Fixed-size chunking splits at fixed token/character counts regardless of content. Semantic chunking produces topically coherent chunks but requires more computation. Fixed-size chunking is simpler and faster but may split mid-topic.
questions.whyCorrect: Semantic chunking uses semantic similarity to determine split points, while fixed-size chunking uses mechanical character/token counts.

whyOthersIncorrect.They produce identical results: They can produce very different chunks, especially on varied content.
whyOthersIncorrect.Semantic chunking is always faster: It requires additional embedding computation during chunking.
whyOthersIncorrect.Fixed-size chunking is more accurate: Fixed-size is simpler but not more semantically accurate.

questions.realWorldExample: A research paper where semantic chunking splits between methodology and results sections, while fixed-size chunking might split in the middle of a paragraph.
commonMistakes: Using semantic chunking without tuning similarity thresholds
Assuming semantic chunking eliminates the need for overlap
bestPractices: Use semantic chunking for unstructured, long-form content
Profile the computational cost of semantic chunking for large document sets

questions.question: How does metadata filtering work in RAG retrieval?
questions.answer: Metadata filtering restricts search results based on non-vector attributes attached to document chunks (date, source, category, author). During retrieval, the system first filters chunks matching metadata criteria, then performs similarity search within that filtered subset. This enables precise, context-aware retrieval such as searching only recent documents or within a specific department.
questions.whyCorrect: Metadata filtering adds structured constraints on top of semantic search, narrowing results to a relevant subset before or during vector similarity computation.

whyOthersIncorrect.Metadata filtering replaces vector searc: Metadata filtering constrains the search space but does not replace similarity matching.
whyOthersIncorrect.Metadata is only useful during indexing: Metadata is most powerful at query time for filtering.
whyOthersIncorrect.All vector databases support the same me: Filtering capabilities vary significantly across vector databases.

questions.realWorldExample: A support bot filtering chunks to only the 'billing' category for payment-related questions, improving relevance.
commonMistakes: Not attaching metadata during index building
Over-filtering and excluding relevant results
bestPractices: Design metadata schema before index building
Use metadata to boost relevance without over-constraining retrieval

questions.question: What is query processing in RAG and what steps does it involve?
questions.answer: Query processing transforms a raw user query into an optimized form for retrieval. Key steps include: (1) query rewriting—clarifying or rephrasing, (2) query expansion—adding synonyms or related terms, (3) query decomposition—breaking complex queries into sub-queries, (4) intent classification—determining query type, (5) embedding the processed query, and (6) applying metadata filters.
questions.whyCorrect: Query processing bridges the gap between how users express questions and how the retrieval system finds relevant documents.

whyOthersIncorrect.Query processing is just embedding the r: Processing involves multiple transformation steps before embedding.
whyOthersIncorrect.Query processing is optional: While optional, it significantly improves retrieval quality.
whyOthersIncorrect.Query processing only happens at query t: Some steps can leverage pre-computed resources.

questions.realWorldExample: An LLM rewrites 'Why won't my thing turn on?' into 'Troubleshooting device power failure causes' for better retrieval.
commonMistakes: Skipping query processing and using raw user queries
Over-processing queries and losing the user's original intent
bestPractices: Use LLM-based query rewriting as a first step
Evaluate which processing steps improve retrieval for your use case

questions.question: How do you optimize chunk size for a RAG system?
questions.answer: Chunk size optimization balances multiple trade-offs: Small chunks (100-300 tokens) improve retrieval precision but may lack context. Large chunks (500-1500 tokens) provide richer context but may dilute relevance. The optimal size depends on embedding model capabilities, document structure, query complexity, and LLM context window. Testing with domain-specific evaluation sets is essential.
questions.whyCorrect: Chunk size directly affects both retrieval quality and generation quality, requiring empirical evaluation.

whyOthersIncorrect.There is one universal optimal chunk siz: Optimal chunk size varies by document type, embedding model, and use case.
whyOthersIncorrect.Bigger chunks are always better for cont: Larger chunks may include irrelevant content that dilutes the signal.
whyOthersIncorrect.Chunk size doesn't affect retrieval qual: It significantly impacts both retrieval precision and answer quality.

questions.realWorldExample: A legal RAG system using 800-token chunks for detailed clause-level retrieval, vs. a news system using 200-token chunks for precise fact extraction.
commonMistakes: Using default chunk sizes without testing
Not considering embedding model token limits when choosing chunk size
bestPractices: Run systematic evaluations across chunk sizes (200, 500, 1000 tokens)
Match chunk size to your typical query granularity

questions.question: What is the RAGAS framework and how does it evaluate RAG systems?
questions.answer: RAGAS (Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment) is an open-source framework for evaluating RAG pipelines. It provides automated metrics including: (1) Faithfulness—scoring whether the answer is supported by context, (2) Answer Relevance—measuring alignment between question and answer, (3) Context Precision—assessing retrieved context relevance, (4) Context Recall—checking if key information was retrieved. It uses LLMs to generate evaluation scores without requiring labeled ground truth.
questions.whyCorrect: RAGAS provides a comprehensive, LLM-powered evaluation suite specifically designed for RAG systems.

whyOthersIncorrect.RAGAS is a vector database: RAGAS is an evaluation framework, not a storage system.
whyOthersIncorrect.RAGAS requires labeled test data: It can evaluate using LLM-based scoring without ground truth labels.
whyOthersIncorrect.RAGAS only evaluates retrieval: It evaluates both retrieval and generation components.

questions.realWorldExample: A team using RAGAS to compare two chunking strategies by measuring faithfulness and context recall across their test set.
commonMistakes: Using RAGAS scores without understanding what each metric measures
Over-relying on automated metrics without human validation
bestPractices: Use RAGAS for systematic evaluation during RAG development
Combine RAGAS metrics with periodic human evaluation for calibration

questions.question: What is source attribution in RAG and why is it important?
questions.answer: Source attribution includes references to specific retrieved documents supporting each part of the generated answer. It builds user trust by allowing verification, enables fact-checking, provides transparency in high-stakes domains (legal, medical), helps users navigate to original sources, and supports compliance requiring citation of sources.
questions.whyCorrect: Source attribution connects generated claims back to their supporting evidence, enabling verification and building trust.

whyOthersIncorrect.Source attribution is only needed in aca: It is valuable in any domain where trust and verification matter.
whyOthersIncorrect.It makes the answer less readable: Well-designed attribution enhances readability and trust.
whyOthersIncorrect.LLMs naturally provide accurate citation: LLMs can generate inaccurate citations; attribution requires explicit engineering.

questions.realWorldExample: A legal research tool citing specific case law paragraphs for each claim in its generated summary.
commonMistakes: Citing sources that don't actually support the generated claim
Making citations too verbose and disrupting readability
bestPractices: Include specific passage references, not just document titles
Verify citations are accurately matched to claims during evaluation

questions.question: How does caching work in RAG systems and why is it used?
questions.answer: Caching stores results of previous queries (embeddings, retrieved chunks, or full responses) to avoid redundant computation. Types include: (1) embedding caching, (2) retrieval caching, (3) response caching. Caching reduces latency, lowers API/embedding costs, and improves throughput for repeated or similar queries.
questions.whyCorrect: Caching avoids redundant embedding computation, retrieval, and LLM generation for repeated queries.

whyOthersIncorrect.Caching is not needed for small systems: Even small systems benefit from caching for cost and latency reduction.
whyOthersIncorrect.Caching always returns stale results: Cache invalidation strategies can keep cached results fresh.
whyOthersIncorrect.Caching only applies to the LLM response: Embedding and retrieval caching are equally important.

questions.realWorldExample: A FAQ bot caching embeddings and responses for the 1000 most common questions, serving 80% of queries from cache.
commonMistakes: Not implementing cache invalidation for updated documents
Caching too aggressively and serving stale answers
bestPractices: Implement semantic caching for similar queries, not just exact matches
Set appropriate TTLs and invalidation strategies for cached results

questions.question: How do you stream RAG responses and why is it beneficial?
questions.answer: Streaming sends tokens to the user as they are generated rather than waiting for the complete response. Implementation involves retrieving context, constructing the prompt, using the LLM's streaming API to yield tokens incrementally, and forwarding via SSE or WebSocket. Benefits include reduced perceived latency, improved user experience for long responses, and the ability to display typing indicators.
questions.whyCorrect: Streaming reduces time-to-first-token and improves perceived performance.

whyOthersIncorrect.Streaming makes the total response faste: It reduces perceived latency but total generation time is similar.
whyOthersIncorrect.Streaming is not compatible with RAG: Streaming works seamlessly with RAG; retrieval happens before streaming starts.
whyOthersIncorrect.Streaming requires a different LLM: Most modern LLM APIs support streaming natively.

questions.realWorldExample: A chatbot that starts showing the answer character-by-character while the LLM generates a 500-word response.
commonMistakes: Not handling streaming errors gracefully
Implementing streaming at the wrong abstraction layer
bestPractices: Use SSE for simple streaming implementations
Ensure error handling gracefully falls back to non-streaming on failure

questions.question: What strategies can reduce costs in a RAG system?
questions.answer: Cost reduction strategies include: (1) Semantic caching, (2) Using smaller/cheaper embedding models, (3) Chunk size optimization, (4) Reducing top-k to minimize LLM input tokens, (5) Using local/self-hosted models, (6) Batch processing for index updates, (7) Metadata filtering to reduce search scope, (8) Using smaller LLMs for simple queries (routing), and (9) Compressing retrieved context before passing to the LLM.
questions.whyCorrect: RAG costs span embeddings, vector DB, LLM calls, and infrastructure—optimization at each layer contributes to overall cost reduction.

whyOthersIncorrect.Only LLM costs matter: Embedding, vector DB, and infrastructure costs are also significant.
whyOthersIncorrect.Cost reduction always hurts quality: Many strategies (caching, chunk optimization) improve both cost and performance.
whyOthersIncorrect.Using the cheapest model is always best: The cheapest model may produce poor quality, requiring more iterations.

questions.realWorldExample: A company reducing embedding costs 60% by switching from OpenAI API to a self-hosted sentence-transformers model.
commonMistakes: Optimizing only LLM costs while ignoring embedding and retrieval costs
Over-optimizing and degrading retrieval quality
bestPractices: Profile costs across all RAG components before optimizing
Measure quality impact of every cost reduction strategy

questions.question: How do you handle document updates and deletions in a RAG system?
questions.answer: Handling document changes requires: (1) Incremental indexing—updating only changed chunks, (2) Document versioning—tracking versions for rollbacks, (3) Chunk ID management—storing unique IDs for specific chunk updates, (4) Deletion handling—removing all chunks of a deleted document, (5) Metadata-based management using document_id to group chunks, (6) Re-embedding only changed sections, and (7) Cache invalidation for affected queries.
questions.whyCorrect: Production RAG systems must handle document lifecycle efficiently without full index rebuilds.

whyOthersIncorrect.Full index rebuild is always safest: It is slow, expensive, and impractical for frequently changing data.
whyOthersIncorrect.Document updates don't affect the index: Outdated chunks lead to stale, incorrect retrieval results.
whyOthersIncorrect.Deleting a document automatically remove: Many vector DBs require explicit chunk deletion by ID.

questions.realWorldExample: A product catalog RAG system where updated prices trigger re-embedding only the changed product chunks, not all 500K products.
commonMistakes: Not tracking which chunks belong to which document
Forgetting to invalidate caches after document updates
bestPractices: Assign unique IDs to every chunk for granular updates
Implement document-level metadata to enable bulk operations

questions.question: How do you handle queries that cannot be answered from the knowledge base?
questions.answer: Handling unanswerable queries requires: (1) Clear prompt instructions to say 'I don't know' when context is insufficient, (2) Confidence scoring using retrieval similarity scores, (3) Threshold-based rejection for low-confidence matches, (4) Fallback strategies—routing to general LLM, human support, or rephrasing suggestions, (5) Logging unanswerable queries to identify knowledge gaps, and (6) Feedback loops to improve the knowledge base.
questions.whyCorrect: A robust RAG system must gracefully handle cases where the knowledge base doesn't contain the answer.

whyOthersIncorrect.The LLM will always find something to sa: Without guardrails, LLMs may hallucinate answers to unanswerable queries.
whyOthersIncorrect.Unanswerable queries are rare: Many queries fall outside the knowledge base scope.
whyOthersIncorrect.Just returning empty results is sufficie: Users need clear communication about why the query couldn't be answered.

questions.realWorldExample: A support bot detecting no matching documents for a discontinued product and responding with 'I don't have information about that. Would you like to contact support?'
commonMistakes: Forgetting to include 'I don't know' instructions in the prompt
Not tracking unanswerable queries to identify knowledge gaps
bestPractices: Set retrieval similarity thresholds for confidence scoring
Log and analyze unanswerable queries to improve the knowledge base

questions.question: How does chunk overlap percentage affect retrieval quality?
questions.answer: Chunk overlap determines how much adjacent chunks share text. Low overlap (0-5%) risks losing boundary context but keeps index small. Medium overlap (10-20%) balances context preservation with efficiency. High overlap (30%+) ensures strong context continuity but increases storage and may introduce redundancy. Optimal overlap depends on document characteristics—narrative text benefits more than tabular data.
questions.whyCorrect: Overlap directly affects the trade-off between context completeness at boundaries and index efficiency/cost.

whyOthersIncorrect.Overlap has no effect on retrieval quali: Overlap directly affects whether boundary-crossing information is retrievable.
whyOthersIncorrect.More overlap always improves results: Excessive overlap increases cost and may degrade retrieval.
whyOthersIncorrect.A fixed 20% overlap works for all use ca: Optimal overlap varies by document type and query patterns.

questions.realWorldExample: A legal document with 15% overlap ensuring that a clause spanning two chunks is fully captured in at least one chunk.
commonMistakes: Using zero overlap without realizing boundary information is lost
Using the same overlap percentage for all document types without testing
bestPractices: Start with 10-20% overlap and evaluate impact
Test overlap impact on queries that span chunk boundaries

questions.question: How do you build a RAG system that supports follow-up questions?
questions.answer: Supporting follow-up questions requires: (1) Conversation history management, (2) Query rewriting to resolve anaphora (e.g., 'What about their pricing?' → 'What is Company X's pricing?'), (3) Context carry-over from previous turns, (4) Multi-turn prompt design formatting history alongside new retrieval results, and (5) Session-aware caching to maintain context across turns.
questions.whyCorrect: Follow-up questions contain implicit references that require conversation history to resolve before effective retrieval.

whyOthersIncorrect.Simply including chat history in the pro: Query rewriting is also needed for effective retrieval.
whyOthersIncorrect.Conversation history is only needed for: It is also needed for query rewriting before retrieval.

questions.realWorldExample: User asks 'Tell me about Tesla's Model 3' then 'What about its range?'—the system rewrites to 'What is the range of Tesla Model 3?' for effective retrieval.
commonMistakes: Not rewriting follow-up queries to include explicit references
Retrieving for the literal follow-up query without conversation context
bestPractices: Use LLM-based query rewriting to resolve anaphora
Limit conversation history to recent turns to avoid context window bloat

questions.question: What is the difference between extractive and generative answers in RAG?
questions.answer: Extractive RAG selects and presents exact passages from retrieved documents. Generative RAG uses the LLM to synthesize new text from retrieved context, combining information from multiple sources. Extractive is more faithful but limited in expressiveness. Generative provides fluent answers but risks hallucination. Many systems use generative with grounding instructions as a middle ground.
questions.whyCorrect: Extractive and generative represent a fidelity vs. fluency trade-off in how retrieved information is presented.

whyOthersIncorrect.They always produce the same answer: They can differ significantly—generative synthesizes, extractive quotes directly.
whyOthersIncorrect.Extractive always prevents hallucination: It reduces it but doesn't eliminate it if irrelevant passages are extracted.
whyOthersIncorrect.Generative is always better: Natural-sounding text can still be hallucinated or inaccurate.

questions.realWorldExample: Extractive: returning the exact warranty paragraph. Generative: synthesizing a summary comparing warranty terms across multiple products.
commonMistakes: Choosing generative when extractive would be safer for compliance
Not evaluating both approaches on the same evaluation dataset
bestPractices: Use extractive for compliance-critical domains
Use generative with grounding instructions for comprehensive answers

questions.question: How do you handle very long documents in a RAG pipeline?
questions.answer: Strategies include: (1) Hierarchical chunking—splitting by sections, subsections, paragraphs, (2) Summary-based indexing—creating summaries alongside full content, (3) Multi-granularity indexing at document and chunk levels, (4) Map-reduce for retrieval—retrieving relevant sections then combining, (5) Sliding window with overlap, (6) Metadata-rich chunking preserving section headings, and (7) Using long-context LLMs for larger chunks.
questions.whyCorrect: Long documents require special handling to ensure all content is accessible while maintaining retrieval precision.

whyOthersIncorrect.Long-context LLMs eliminate chunking: Even with large windows, retrieval of relevant sections improves precision.
whyOthersIncorrect.Only the first few pages need indexing: Important information may be anywhere in a document.

questions.realWorldExample: A 200-page technical manual indexed hierarchically: document → chapters → sections → paragraphs with metadata.
commonMistakes: Ignoring document structure when splitting
Not preserving section context in chunk metadata
bestPractices: Preserve hierarchical structure in chunk metadata
Use summary-based indexing for very long documents

questions.question: What is the difference between pre-filtering and post-filtering in vector search?
questions.answer: Pre-filtering applies metadata filters before vector search, narrowing the search space. Post-filtering performs vector search across all documents, then filters by metadata after ranking. Pre-filtering is more efficient but may miss relevant documents. Post-filtering preserves retrieval quality but may return fewer results than requested.
questions.whyCorrect: Pre-filtering and post-filtering trade off between search efficiency and retrieval completeness.

whyOthersIncorrect.They produce identical results: Pre-filtering may miss relevant documents that would have ranked highly.
whyOthersIncorrect.Pre-filtering is always more efficient: High-selectivity pre-filters that remove few documents may add overhead.
whyOthersIncorrect.Post-filtering is always better: It may return fewer results than top-k requires.

questions.realWorldExample: Product search filtering to 'electronics': pre-filtering searches only electronics embeddings (fast), post-filtering searches all then keeps electronics (complete but slower).
commonMistakes: Using pre-filtering when the filter is very selective
Not handling cases where post-filtering returns fewer results than top-k
bestPractices: Use pre-filtering for highly selective filters
Use post-filtering when filter selectivity is low and quality matters more

questions.question: What is the difference between a bi-encoder and a cross-encoder in RAG?
questions.answer: A bi-encoder encodes query and document separately into independent embeddings, enabling fast search via pre-computed document embeddings. A cross-encoder jointly encodes query and document as a single input, producing more accurate relevance scores but requiring encoding each query-document pair at query time. Bi-encoders are used for initial retrieval (fast, scalable); cross-encoders for re-ranking (slow, accurate).
questions.whyCorrect: Bi-encoders optimize for speed by encoding independently; cross-encoders optimize for accuracy by encoding jointly.

whyOthersIncorrect.They are the same architecture: They differ fundamentally in how they process query-document pairs.
whyOthersIncorrect.Cross-encoders can replace bi-encoders a: Cross-encoders are too slow for full-index search.
whyOthersIncorrect.Bi-encoders are always less accurate: Bi-encoders with good training can approach cross-encoder accuracy for many tasks.

questions.realWorldExample: A RAG system using OpenAI embeddings (bi-encoder) for initial retrieval, then ms-marco-MiniLM (cross-encoder) for re-ranking top-20 results.
commonMistakes: Using cross-encoders for full-index search (impractical)
Not measuring the quality improvement from cross-encoder re-ranking
bestPractices: Use bi-encoders for retrieval, cross-encoders for re-ranking
Measure the latency vs quality trade-off of cross-encoder use

questions.question: What is multi-modal RAG and how does it extend traditional RAG?
questions.answer: Multi-modal RAG extends traditional text-based RAG by retrieving and reasoning over multiple data types—images, tables, audio, video—alongside text. It uses multi-modal embedding models (e.g., CLIP) to create shared embedding spaces for different modalities, enabling cross-modal retrieval. The generator must also handle multi-modal inputs.
questions.whyCorrect: Multi-modal RAG enables retrieval across different data types using shared embedding spaces.

whyOthersIncorrect.Multi-modal RAG is just text RAG with im: It fundamentally changes retrieval to work across modalities.
whyOthersIncorrect.Traditional embedding models support mul: Most text embedding models cannot process images; specialized models like CLIP are needed.
whyOthersIncorrect.Multi-modal RAG requires no changes to t: The generator must handle and reason over multi-modal context.

questions.realWorldExample: A medical imaging RAG system that retrieves relevant X-ray images and their radiology reports together for a doctor's query.
commonMistakes: Assuming text embedding models work for images
Ignoring the complexity of multi-modal prompt construction
bestPractices: Use models like CLIP for shared text-image embedding spaces
Design multi-modal prompts that clearly present different content types

questions.question: What is Agentic RAG and how does it differ from standard RAG?
questions.answer: Agentic RAG integrates an LLM agent into the retrieval pipeline, enabling dynamic, multi-step retrieval and reasoning. Unlike standard RAG (single retrieval + generation), Agentic RAG can: (1) decide whether retrieval is needed, (2) formulate multiple queries iteratively, (3) use different tools and data sources, (4) evaluate retrieved content and retrieve again if insufficient, and (5) combine reasoning with retrieval in a loop.
questions.whyCorrect: Agentic RAG treats retrieval as an iterative, decision-making process orchestrated by an agent.

whyOthersIncorrect.Agentic RAG is just standard RAG with be: It fundamentally changes the architecture to include agent loops.
whyOthersIncorrect.Agentic RAG always performs better: It adds latency and complexity; simpler RAG suffices for straightforward queries.
whyOthersIncorrect.Agentic RAG does not use retrieval: Retrieval remains a core component; the agent controls when and how to retrieve.

questions.realWorldExample: An agent that first searches company docs for a refund policy, then queries the CRM for order history, combining both for a complex support request.
commonMistakes: Over-engineering with agents when simple RAG suffices
Not setting guardrails for agent retrieval loops
bestPractices: Use Agentic RAG for complex, multi-step queries
Implement iteration limits and evaluation criteria for the agent

questions.question: What are the key metrics for evaluating RAG system quality?
questions.answer: Key metrics include: (1) Faithfulness—whether the answer is grounded in context, (2) Answer relevance—whether the answer addresses the query, (3) Context precision—whether retrieved chunks are relevant, (4) Context recall—whether all necessary information was retrieved, (5) Retrieval precision/recall—traditional IR metrics, and (6) End-to-end accuracy. Tools like RAGAS provide automated evaluation.
questions.whyCorrect: RAG systems must be evaluated at both retrieval and generation stages using metrics capturing grounding, relevance, and completeness.

whyOthersIncorrect.Only accuracy matters: Accuracy alone doesn't distinguish hallucination from correct grounding.
whyOthersIncorrect.Human evaluation is unnecessary: Automated metrics complement but don't replace human evaluation.
whyOthersIncorrect.BLEU score is sufficient: BLEU measures text similarity, not faithfulness or retrieval quality.

questions.realWorldExample: Using RAGAS to evaluate a RAG system's faithfulness and context recall across 500 test queries.
commonMistakes: Evaluating only end-to-end accuracy without component-level metrics
Not establishing human evaluation baselines for metric calibration
bestPractices: Evaluate both retrieval and generation independently
Use RAGAS or similar frameworks for systematic evaluation

questions.question: How can hallucination occur in RAG systems and how can it be mitigated?
questions.answer: Hallucination in RAG occurs when the LLM generates information not supported by retrieved context. Causes: (1) context doesn't contain the answer but LLM generates one anyway, (2) irrelevant/noisy chunks mislead the model, (3) model prioritizes parametric knowledge over context, (4) ambiguous queries lead to mismatched retrieval. Mitigations: grounding prompts, confidence scoring, re-ranking, fact-checking post-generation, and faithfulness evaluation.
questions.whyCorrect: RAG reduces but does not eliminate hallucination; multiple mitigation layers are needed.

whyOthersIncorrect.RAG completely eliminates hallucination: RAG reduces but doesn't eliminate hallucination.
whyOthersIncorrect.Hallucination is always caused by poor r: It can also arise from the LLM ignoring good context.
whyOthersIncorrect.Grounding prompts alone solve hallucinat: They help but multi-layered mitigation is needed.

questions.realWorldExample: A financial RAG system where the LLM invents a stock price not in the retrieved report, caught by a faithfulness checker.
commonMistakes: Assuming RAG alone prevents hallucination
Not implementing post-generation fact-checking
bestPractices: Use grounding instructions in the RAG prompt
Implement faithfulness scoring to detect hallucinated content

questions.question: What is the 'lost-in-the-middle' phenomenon in RAG?
questions.answer: Lost-in-the-middle refers to LLMs paying less attention to information in the middle of a long context, preferring content at the beginning and end. In RAG, chunks placed in the middle of the context window may be ignored even if most relevant. Mitigations: placing the most relevant chunk first, re-ordering by relevance, reducing retrieved chunks, and using models trained for long contexts.
questions.whyCorrect: LLMs exhibit positional bias in attention, making boundary content more salient than middle content.

whyOthersIncorrect.Lost-in-the-middle only affects very lon: It can affect contexts as short as 4-5 chunks.
whyOthersIncorrect.All LLMs handle middle context equally w: Studies show significant attention drop-off for middle positions.
whyOthersIncorrect.Randomizing chunk order solves the probl: It may help but doesn't eliminate fundamental attention bias.
